title: V. Dhanapal Chettiar v. Yesodai Ammal
text: Constitution Bench holding that a notice to quit under Section 106 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882 is NOT necessary to seek eviction under State Rent Control legislation. Once a State Rent Act governs the tenancy, the landlord need only establish a ground of eviction under that Act; a valid or invalid s.106 notice is irrelevant. Directly addresses defences based on defective quit notices in eviction suits.

title: K. Bhaskaran v. Sankaran Vaidhyan Balan
text: Leading authority on Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881. Explains the five components of the s.138 offence (drawing, presentation, return unpaid, demand notice, failure to pay) and holds that the offence is complete only on default after notice. Also foundational on territorial jurisdiction for cheque-dishonour complaints.

title: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar
text: Landmark on arrest in offences punishable up to seven years, especially Section 498A IPC and Section 4 Dowry Prohibition Act. Directs police not to arrest automatically and to follow Section 41 and 41A CrPC; magistrates must not authorise detention mechanically. Central authority against unjustified arrests and for anticipatory relief in matrimonial cruelty cases.

title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
text: Expanded Article 21: 'procedure established by law' must be just, fair and reasonable, not arbitrary. Read Articles 14, 19 and 21 together as a golden triangle. Foundational precedent for due process, natural justice, and reasonableness of State action affecting life and personal liberty.

title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India
text: Nine-judge Bench holding that the right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21 and Part III of the Constitution. Any invasion must satisfy legality, necessity and proportionality. Governs data protection, surveillance, bodily autonomy and informational self-determination claims.

title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
text: Laid down binding guidelines against sexual harassment of women at the workplace in the absence of legislation, treating it as a violation of Articles 14, 19 and 21. The Vishaka Guidelines governed employer duties and complaint mechanisms until the 2013 POSH Act. Key precedent for workplace harassment and employer liability.

title: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
text: Held that a divorced Muslim woman is entitled to maintenance under Section 125 CrPC, a secular provision applying regardless of personal law, if she is unable to maintain herself. Landmark on maintenance rights and the interface between Section 125 CrPC and Muslim personal law.

title: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
text: Held that the right to livelihood is an integral part of the right to life under Article 21, so pavement dwellers could not be evicted without following a fair procedure. Key authority on eviction of the urban poor, procedural fairness, and the reach of Article 21.

title: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak)
text: Evolved the principle of absolute liability for enterprises engaged in hazardous activities, going beyond the Rylands v. Fletcher strict-liability rule and its exceptions. An enterprise that causes harm from a hazardous activity is absolutely liable to compensate, with damages calibrated to its capacity. Central to environmental and mass-tort liability.

title: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India
text: Struck down Section 66A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 as unconstitutional for violating Article 19(1)(a), being vague and overbroad. Read down intermediary liability under Section 79. Leading authority on online free speech, chilling effect, and vagueness/overbreadth doctrine.

title: Bharat Aluminium Co. v. Kaiser Aluminium (BALCO)
text: Constitution Bench holding that Part I of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 does not apply to arbitrations seated outside India; Indian courts cannot grant interim relief or set aside foreign-seated awards under Part I. Seat of arbitration determines the supervisory jurisdiction. Foundational on international commercial arbitration and the seat theory.

title: Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary, State of Bihar
text: Held that the right to a speedy trial is implicit in Article 21 and that undertrial prisoners detained beyond the maximum sentence must be released. Recognised the right to free legal aid for the indigent accused. Key precedent on undertrial detention, bail, and access to justice.

title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India
text: Upheld 27% reservation for Other Backward Classes but capped total reservations at 50%, introduced the 'creamy layer' exclusion, and barred reservation in promotions (later modified by amendment). Leading authority on Articles 15(4) and 16(4), backward-class identification, and the reservation ceiling.

title: Hiralal Kapur v. Prabhu Choudhury
text: Addresses eviction on the ground of wilful default in payment of rent and the tenant's opportunity to deposit arrears. Discusses when default is 'wilful' and the consequences of non-payment under rent-control law. Relevant to eviction suits founded on non-payment of rent and statutory tenancy defences.
